local_names.romanized_local: Si gua (Mandarin); Petola segi (Malay); Peerkkangai (Tamil); Torai (Hindi)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for angled loofah / ridge gourd in available terminology.

serving.description: 1 medium ridge gourd (approx. 200 g edible portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw angled loofah is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one medium-sized ridge gourd after trimming ends and removing the ridged skin.

nutrient_source.title: Angled Loofah, Raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for angled loofah, raw (cr_id F14040012). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Luffa, raw (FDC ID 168430) for secondary nutrient fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on non-starchy gourd vegetables (e.g., bitter gourd, zucchini, cucumber; GI 15–32)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for angled loofah (Luffa acutangula) found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous non-starchy gourd vegetables (bitter gourd GI ~18, zucchini GI ~15, cucumber GI ~15, pumpkin GI ~75 excluded as starchy). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (25 × 3.65 × 2) / 100 = 1.83, rounded to 1.8. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium content (107 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Angled loofah is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content and moderate potassium supply support the DASH dietary pattern, which targets a sodium-to-potassium ratio favourable for blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.65 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~25), and low glycemic load (~1.8 per 200 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of under 2 per standard serving, raw angled loofah has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for inclusion in a carbohydrate-controlled diet for type 2 diabetes management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Angled loofah is virtually fat-free with no cholesterol and provides dietary fibre that contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction; regular consumption as part of a vegetable-rich diet is consistent with cardiovascular disease prevention guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Provides vitamin C and dietary fibre but lacks concentrated anti-inflammatory factors
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Angled loofah provides vitamin C and dietary fibre that support gut health and immune modulation; however, it does not contain concentrated anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or >3 g whole-food fibre per 100 g, so it should be considered a neutral rather than therapeutic food for rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Angled loofah (Luffa acutangula), also known as ridge gourd or Chinese okra, is a tropical vine vegetable widely consumed across South and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, India, Malaysia, and China. The raw fruit has a distinctive ridged green skin, firm and crisp white flesh, and a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a subtle vegetal note. It is botanically distinct from the smooth loofah (Luffa cylindrica) and is harvested young before the fibrous interior matures. In Singapore and Chinese cuisine it is called 丝瓜 (si gua) and is commonly stir-fried, steamed, or added to soups; in South Asian cooking it is known as torai (Hindi) or peerkkangai (Tamil) and is prepared as a dry sabzi or curry. Nutritionally, raw angled loofah is very low in calories (21 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (3.65 g/100 g), with negligible fat (0.07 g/100 g) and modest protein (0.81 g/100 g). It provides dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g), potassium (107 mg/100 g), calcium (10 mg/100 g), iron (0.25 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (~12 mg/100 g). Its very low sodium content (4 mg/100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (~25) make it suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The high water content (~94 g/100 g) contributes to satiety and hydration. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is Chinese okra good for diabetes?
Can I eat ridge gourd if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of loofah?
How many calories in 100g of ridge gourd?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040012, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrient fields cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for Luffa, raw (FDC ID 168430). GI is an analogous estimate from approved GI source (University of Sydney GI Database) as no direct measurement exists; this is documented and does not reduce overall confidence below high for the nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; angled loofah is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are 0.0 — naturally zero as this is an unfortified plant food. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero in raw plant foods. 2. Saturated fat (0.02 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.03 g), sugar (1.80 g), vitamin C (12 mg), vitamin A (4 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168430 (Luffa, raw) as HPB does not report these fields; this is documented as an adapted match. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy gourd vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Luffa acutangula was found in the database as of April 2026. 4. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this has been retained in dietary_flags context but not as a formal flag, as the carbohydrate content (3.65 g/100 g) is low but the food is not specifically formulated for ketogenic diets. 5. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable: false with a contradictory reason; this has been corrected to concern_level: low with a neutral clinical note. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168430 as HPB does not report these fields.

Added saturated_fat_g, sugar_g from USDA FDC 168430 to satisfy Q3 secondary nutrient completeness.
Added GI estimate (25, low) with analogous source documentation to satisfy Q4.

Populated aliases_by_language and verified consistency with flat aliases array to satisfy Q8.
